question_text: Which of the following is NOT a structural gene of the lac operon?
explanation: The lac operon consists of three structural genes: lacZ (codes for beta-galactosidase), lacY (codes for permease), and lacA (codes for transacetylase). The regulator gene (i gene) is not part of the operon itself; it is located upstream and codes for the repressor protein that regulates the operon. The i gene is constitutively expressed and produces the repressor even in the absence of an inducer.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: lacZ is one of the three structural genes of the lac operon. It codes for the enzyme beta-galactosidase.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing structural genes with regulatory elements

elimination_hints.hint: lacY is a structural gene of the lac operon that codes for permease, the enzyme responsible for transporting lactose into the cell.
lacA is a structural gene of the lac operon that codes for transacetylase.

question_text: The enzyme beta-galactosidase, encoded by the lac operon, catalyzes the hydrolysis of lactose into:
explanation: Beta-galactosidase (encoded by lacZ) is the key enzyme of the lac operon. It hydrolyzes the disaccharide lactose into its two monosaccharide components: galactose and glucose. Additionally, beta-galactosidase can also convert lactose into allolactose (an isomer of lactose), which acts as the actual inducer of the lac operon.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Glucose and fructose are the products of sucrose hydrolysis by the enzyme sucrase/invertase, not lactose hydrolysis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing products of sucrose hydrolysis with lactose hydrolysis

elimination_hints.hint: Fructose is not a component of lactose. Lactose is a disaccharide made of galactose and glucose.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the monosaccharide composition of lactose with other disaccharides

elimination_hints.hint: Maltose is a disaccharide of two glucose units. It is not a product of lactose hydrolysis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different disaccharide breakdown products

question_text: The Human Genome Project (HGP) was coordinated by which of the following institutions?
explanation: The Human Genome Project (HGP), launched in 1990 and completed in 2003, was a 13-year international collaborative effort. It was coordinated jointly by the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the National Institutes of Health (NIH). The project aimed to identify all human genes, determine the sequence of the 3.1 billion base pairs, and store this information in databases.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: U.S. Department of Energy and National Institutes of Health

elimination_hints.hint: WHO and UNESCO are international organizations focused on health policy and education respectively. They were not involved in coordinating HGP.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing international health organizations with the agencies that coordinated the HGP

elimination_hints.hint: NSF and CSIR are research bodies, but the HGP was specifically coordinated by DOE and NIH.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing general research funding agencies with the specific coordinators of HGP

elimination_hints.hint: FDA regulates food and drugs, while CDC handles disease control. Neither coordinated the Human Genome Project.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing US health regulatory agencies with genome research coordinators

question_text: In the lac operon, the site where RNA polymerase binds to initiate transcription of structural genes is the:
explanation: The promoter is the DNA sequence where RNA polymerase binds to initiate transcription of the structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA) of the lac operon. The operator is the region where the repressor protein binds to block transcription. The regulator gene (i gene) codes for the repressor protein, and the inducer (allolactose) binds to the repressor to inactivate it. The promoter is located upstream of the operator and structural genes.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The operator is the DNA sequence where the repressor protein binds, not where RNA polymerase binds. When the repressor binds the operator, it physically blocks RNA polymerase from proceeding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the binding site for the repressor with that for RNA polymerase

elimination_hints.hint: The regulator gene (i gene) codes for the repressor protein. It is not the site where RNA polymerase binds for structural gene transcription.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the regulatory gene with the promoter region

elimination_hints.hint: Structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA) are the regions that get transcribed, not the binding site for RNA polymerase.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the transcribed region with the transcription initiation site

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes negative regulation of the lac operon?
explanation: The lac operon exhibits both negative and positive regulation. In negative regulation, the repressor protein (produced by the i gene) binds to the operator region and prevents RNA polymerase from transcribing the structural genes. This is the default state when lactose is absent. When lactose is present, allolactose (an isomer of lactose) acts as the inducer, binding to the repressor and changing its conformation so it can no longer bind the operator, allowing transcription to proceed.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: RNA polymerase binds to the operator and activates transcription
Allolactose binds to the promoter and blocks RNA polymerase binding
The repressor protein binds to the operator and prevents transcription of structural genes
CAP protein binds to the promoter and inhibits transcription

elimination_hints.hint: RNA polymerase binds to the promoter (not the operator) to initiate transcription. The operator is the binding site for the repressor protein.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the roles of the operator and promoter in lac operon regulation

elimination_hints.hint: Allolactose is the inducer that binds to the repressor protein (not the promoter). It inactivates the repressor, allowing transcription.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the target of the inducer molecule

elimination_hints.hint: CAP (Catabolite Activator Protein) is involved in positive regulation of the lac operon, not negative regulation. It enhances transcription when bound to cAMP.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing positive regulation (CAP-cAMP) with negative regulation (repressor)

question_text: According to the Human Genome Project, the estimated number of genes in the human genome is approximately:
explanation: One of the most surprising findings of the Human Genome Project was that the human genome contains far fewer genes than initially expected. Earlier estimates suggested 80,000 to 100,000 genes, but the HGP revealed that the human genome contains only about 20,000-25,000 genes (NCERT states less than 30,000 genes). This was unexpected because organisms like rice (Oryza sativa) have approximately 32,000-50,000 genes, more than humans.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: 80,000-100,000 was the earlier prediction before the HGP was completed. The actual number turned out to be much lower than anticipated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pre-HGP estimates with the actual findings of the project

elimination_hints.hint: 50,000-60,000 genes is closer to the gene count of rice (Oryza sativa), not humans. Humans have fewer genes than many plants.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the human gene count based on organism complexity

elimination_hints.hint: This vastly overestimates human gene number. The HGP revealed the human genome has far fewer genes than previously assumed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming more complex organisms must have significantly more genes

question_text: In the regulation of the lac operon, when lactose is present in the medium, the inducer molecule that inactivates the repressor is:
explanation: When lactose enters the bacterial cell, a small amount of it is converted to allolactose, which is an isomer of lactose. Allolactose acts as the true inducer of the lac operon. It binds to the repressor protein (produced by the i gene), causing a conformational change in the repressor. This altered repressor can no longer bind to the operator, allowing RNA polymerase to transcribe the structural genes. Lactose itself does not directly act as the inducer.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Although lactose is commonly referred to as the inducer in simplified descriptions, the actual inducer molecule is allolactose, an isomer of lactose formed inside the cell.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming lactose directly acts as the inducer without isomerization

elimination_hints.hint: Galactose is a product of lactose hydrolysis by beta-galactosidase. It is not the inducer of the lac operon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the hydrolysis products of lactose with the inducer

elimination_hints.hint: IPTG (isopropyl-beta-D-thiogalactopyranoside) is a synthetic (artificial) inducer used in laboratory experiments. It is not the natural inducer of the lac operon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing artificial laboratory inducers with the natural biological inducer

question_text: The total number of base pairs in the human genome as revealed by the Human Genome Project is approximately:
explanation: The Human Genome Project revealed that the human genome consists of approximately 3.1 billion (3.1 × 10⁹) base pairs distributed across 23 pairs of chromosomes (22 autosomes + 1 pair of sex chromosomes). Chromosome 1 has the most genes (2968 genes), while the Y chromosome has the fewest (231 genes). The average gene size is approximately 3000 base pairs.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: 3.1 million base pairs is far too small for the human genome. Even the E. coli genome is about 4.6 million base pairs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically underestimating the size of the human genome

elimination_hints.hint: 31 million base pairs is still far too small for the human genome. This is roughly the size of some small eukaryotic genomes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Underestimating the human genome size by two orders of magnitude

elimination_hints.hint: 310 million base pairs is approximately one-tenth of the actual human genome size. The correct value is 3.1 billion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the genome size by one order of magnitude

question_text: The methodology used in the Human Genome Project that involved identifying all genes expressed as mRNA is called:
explanation: The Human Genome Project used two major approaches. One was Expressed Sequence Tags (ESTs), which focused on identifying all the genes expressed as mRNA. ESTs are short sub-sequences of cDNA (complementary DNA synthesized from mRNA). The other approach was Sequence Annotation, which involved sequencing the entire genome first and then assigning functions to different regions. ESTs provided a shortcut to identify coding regions of the genome.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Sequence Annotation is the other HGP approach that involves sequencing the entire genome and then assigning functions to regions. It does not specifically start with mRNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the two methodological approaches of HGP

elimination_hints.hint: DNA Fingerprinting is a technique used for identification of individuals based on differences in repetitive DNA, not a method used in genome sequencing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA identification techniques with genome sequencing methods

elimination_hints.hint: RFLP is a technique that analyzes variations in DNA fragment lengths after restriction enzyme digestion. It is used as a molecular marker, not as a genome sequencing method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing molecular marker techniques with genome sequencing approaches

question_text: In the lac operon model, when the repressor binds to the operator, RNA polymerase cannot access the promoter because:
explanation: In the lac operon, the operator region is located between the promoter and the structural genes and partially overlaps with the promoter. When the repressor protein binds to the operator, it physically obstructs the movement of RNA polymerase from the promoter to the structural genes. This steric hindrance prevents RNA polymerase from transcribing the structural genes, even though it can still bind to the promoter. This is the basis of negative regulation in the lac operon.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: The repressor degrades the RNA polymerase enzyme
The repressor modifies the promoter sequence chemically
The repressor binds to RNA polymerase directly and inhibits it
The repressor bound to the operator physically prevents RNA polymerase from transcribing the structural genes

elimination_hints.hint: The repressor does not degrade RNA polymerase. It simply blocks its passage by binding to the operator region.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming the repressor has proteolytic activity against RNA polymerase

elimination_hints.hint: The repressor does not chemically modify the promoter. It acts by physically binding to the operator to block transcription.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing physical blocking with chemical modification of DNA

elimination_hints.hint: The repressor does not directly interact with RNA polymerase. It binds to the operator DNA and acts as a physical roadblock.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA-binding regulation with direct protein-protein inhibition

question_text: A mutation in the operator region of the lac operon makes it unable to bind the repressor protein. What will be the effect on transcription of the structural genes?
explanation: If the operator has a mutation (called an operator-constitutive or Oᶜ mutation) that prevents repressor binding, the repressor protein can no longer block RNA polymerase from transcribing the structural genes. As a result, the structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA) will be transcribed continuously, regardless of whether lactose is present or absent. This is called constitutive expression because the genes are always 'on'. This demonstrates that the operator is essential for negative regulation of the operon.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: Structural genes will never be transcribed
Structural genes will be transcribed constitutively (continuously)
Structural genes will be transcribed only in the presence of lactose
The repressor will bind to the promoter instead

elimination_hints.hint: If the repressor cannot bind the operator, there is no physical block to RNA polymerase. Therefore, transcription will actually increase, not stop.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly assuming that a dysfunctional operator leads to gene silencing

elimination_hints.hint: Normal (wild-type) regulation requires repressor-operator interaction. Without repressor binding, the genes will be expressed regardless of lactose.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing normal inducible regulation with the consequence of a non-functional operator

elimination_hints.hint: The repressor protein specifically binds to the operator sequence. If the operator is mutated, the repressor cannot bind anywhere else to regulate the operon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming the repressor can switch binding sites when the operator is non-functional

question_text: Which of the following salient features of the Human Genome Project is INCORRECT?
explanation: According to the findings of the Human Genome Project: (1) The human genome has approximately 3.1 billion base pairs. (2) The average gene consists of about 3000 bases, but the largest known human gene is dystrophin with 2.4 million bases. (3) Less than 2% of the genome codes for proteins. (4) Repeated sequences make up a very large portion of the human genome. (5) Chromosome 1 has the most genes (2968), and the Y chromosome has the fewest (231). The statement that more than 50% of the genome codes for proteins is incorrect; in fact, less than 2% of the genome codes for proteins, with the rest being non-coding DNA including introns, repetitive sequences, and regulatory regions.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: Chromosome 1 has the most number of genes (2968)
The largest known human gene is dystrophin at 2.4 million bases
More than 50 percent of the human genome codes for proteins
The average gene consists of about 3000 bases

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct HGP finding. Chromosome 1 is the largest human chromosome and indeed contains the highest number of genes (2968).
This is a correct HGP finding. The dystrophin gene, mutations in which cause Duchenne muscular dystrophy, is indeed the largest known human gene at 2.4 million bases.
This is a correct HGP finding. The average gene size in the human genome is approximately 3000 bases, although sizes vary enormously.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The lac operon is an example of an inducible operon system.
Reason (R): The structural genes of the lac operon are transcribed only when the inducer (allolactose) is present, which inactivates the repressor protein.

Choose the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
explanation: The Assertion is true: the lac operon is indeed an inducible operon system because it is normally switched off and is turned on (induced) only when the substrate (lactose/allolactose) is present. The Reason is also true: the structural genes are transcribed when allolactose binds to the repressor protein, causing a conformational change that prevents the repressor from binding to the operator, thus allowing RNA polymerase to transcribe the genes. The Reason correctly explains the Assertion because the inducible nature of the lac operon is precisely due to the inducer-mediated repressor inactivation mechanism described in R. Hence, option (A) is correct.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R is not merely a separate true fact; it directly explains why the lac operon is inducible. The mechanism in R (inducer inactivating the repressor) is the precise reason for the inducible nature described in A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the causal link between the repressor-inducer mechanism and the inducible nature of the operon

elimination_hints.hint: R is not false. Allolactose does indeed inactivate the repressor protein by binding to it and changing its conformation, allowing transcription of structural genes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly rejecting the role of allolactose in repressor inactivation

elimination_hints.hint: A is not false. The lac operon is a classic example of an inducible operon that is turned on only in the presence of its substrate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing inducible operons with constitutive or repressible operons

question_text:
Assertion (A): In the lac operon, the repressor protein can bind to the operator even in the presence of lactose in the medium.
Reason (R): Allolactose, the actual inducer of the lac operon, is an isomer of lactose that binds to the repressor and changes its conformation so it cannot bind to the operator.

Choose the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false and R is true
explanation: The Assertion is false: when lactose is present, it is converted to allolactose, which binds to the repressor and changes its shape so that it cannot bind to the operator. Therefore, the repressor cannot bind to the operator in the presence of lactose. The Reason is true: allolactose is indeed an isomer of lactose that acts as the inducer by binding to the repressor and causing a conformational change that prevents operator binding. Since A is false and R is true, the correct answer is (D).
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false and R is true

elimination_hints.hint: A is false because when lactose is present, allolactose inactivates the repressor. R is true but it actually explains why A is false, not why it is true.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognize that the inducer prevents repressor-operator binding

elimination_hints.hint: Both A and R are not true. The assertion is false because allolactose (formed from lactose) prevents the repressor from binding to the operator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly accepting the assertion that repressor can bind operator even when lactose is present

elimination_hints.hint: R is not false. Allolactose is indeed an isomer of lactose and the actual inducer that causes conformational change in the repressor protein.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly rejecting the well-established role of allolactose as the natural inducer

question_text:
Match the following components of the lac operon with their functions:

Column I (Component) → Column II (Function)
(P) lacZ → (i) Codes for permease
(Q) lacY → (ii) Codes for beta-galactosidase
(R) lacA → (iii) Codes for repressor protein
(S) i gene → (iv) Codes for transacetylase

Choose the correct match:
(A) P-ii, Q-i, R-iii, S-iv
(B) P-i, Q-ii, R-iv, S-iii
(C) P-ii, Q-i, R-iv, S-iii
(D) P-iv, Q-iii, R-i, S-ii
explanation: The correct matching is: lacZ codes for beta-galactosidase (P-ii), which hydrolyzes lactose into galactose and glucose; lacY codes for permease (Q-i), which facilitates the transport of lactose into the cell; lacA codes for transacetylase (R-iv), which transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to beta-galactosides; and the i gene codes for the repressor protein (S-iii), which binds to the operator to prevent transcription when lactose is absent.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: This match assigns lacA (R) to the repressor protein, which is incorrect. lacA codes for transacetylase, and the i gene codes for the repressor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the function of lacA with the i gene

elimination_hints.hint: This match swaps lacZ and lacY functions. lacZ codes for beta-galactosidase, not permease; lacY codes for permease, not beta-galactosidase.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the products of lacZ and lacY genes

elimination_hints.hint: This option has completely scrambled assignments. None of the component-function pairs are correct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Complete confusion between lac operon gene products

question_text:
Match the following features of the Human Genome Project with their correct details:

Column I (Feature) → Column II (Detail)
(P) Total base pairs → (i) Less than 2 percent
(Q) Coding sequences → (ii) 1.4 million
(R) SNPs (Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms) → (iii) 3.1 billion
(S) Chromosome with most genes → (iv) Chromosome 1

Choose the correct match:
(A) P-iii, Q-i, R-ii, S-iv
(B) P-iii, Q-ii, R-i, S-iv
(C) P-ii, Q-i, R-iii, S-iv
(D) P-iii, Q-i, R-iv, S-ii
explanation: The correct matching is: Total base pairs in the human genome = 3.1 billion (P-iii); Coding sequences (portion that codes for proteins) = less than 2 percent of the genome (Q-i); SNPs (Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms, which are variations at single base pair positions) = approximately 1.4 million identified (R-ii); and the chromosome with the most genes = Chromosome 1, which has 2968 genes (S-iv). These are key salient features of the HGP frequently tested in NEET.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: This option swaps coding sequences with SNPs. Coding sequences are less than 2%, not 1.4 million. SNPs number 1.4 million, not less than 2%.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the percentage of coding DNA with the count of SNPs

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns 1.4 million to total base pairs, which is incorrect. The human genome has 3.1 billion base pairs; 1.4 million refers to the number of SNPs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically underestimating the total base pairs in the human genome

elimination_hints.hint: This option assigns Chromosome 1 to SNPs and 1.4 million to the chromosome with most genes. Chromosome 1 has the most genes (2968), and 1.4 million is the number of SNPs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up numerical features with chromosomal features of HGP

question_text: True or False: The lac operon in E. coli is switched off when lactose is absent from the growth medium because the repressor protein binds to the operator region.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. In the absence of lactose, the i gene produces the repressor protein, which binds to the operator region of the lac operon. When the repressor occupies the operator, RNA polymerase is blocked from transcribing the structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA). This is the default 'off' state of the lac operon. The operon is only turned on when lactose (converted to allolactose) is present to inactivate the repressor.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The statement accurately describes the default off state of the lac operon. When lactose is absent, the repressor binds the operator and blocks transcription.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly thinking the lac operon is always on or that the repressor does not bind the operator in the absence of lactose

question_text: True or False: The Human Genome Project revealed that the Y chromosome has the highest number of genes among all human chromosomes.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. The Human Genome Project revealed that the Y chromosome has the fewest genes among all human chromosomes, with only 231 genes. Chromosome 1, being the largest human chromosome, has the highest number of genes with 2968 genes. The Y chromosome is one of the smallest human chromosomes and carries genes primarily related to male sex determination and spermatogenesis.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The Y chromosome actually has the fewest genes (231) among all human chromosomes, not the highest. Chromosome 1 has the most genes (2968).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the Y chromosome with Chromosome 1 in terms of gene count

question_text: In the Human Genome Project, the technique of ______ was developed by Frederick Sanger and was used for determining the nucleotide sequence of DNA.
explanation: Frederick Sanger developed the dideoxy chain termination method (also known as Sanger sequencing or the chain termination method) for determining the nucleotide sequence of DNA. This technique uses dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs) that lack the 3'-OH group, causing chain termination at specific bases. This method was the foundation for DNA sequencing in the Human Genome Project and earned Sanger his second Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1980. Automated DNA sequencers based on Sanger's method were crucial for completing the HGP.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: PCR was developed by Kary Mullis, not Frederick Sanger. PCR amplifies DNA but does not determine its sequence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA amplification (PCR) with DNA sequencing (Sanger method)

elimination_hints.hint: Southern blotting was developed by Edwin Southern for detecting specific DNA fragments. It is not a sequencing technique.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA detection/hybridization techniques with DNA sequencing methods

elimination_hints.hint: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size but is not a sequencing technique by itself. It is used as a component within the Sanger sequencing method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a separation technique with the complete sequencing methodology

question_text: A researcher is studying a mutant strain of E. coli in which the i gene of the lac operon has been deleted. The bacterium is grown in a medium containing only glucose (no lactose). Which of the following observations would the researcher most likely make regarding the expression of the lac operon structural genes?
explanation: If the i gene is deleted, no repressor protein can be produced. Without the repressor, there is nothing to bind to the operator and block RNA polymerase. Consequently, the structural genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA) will be transcribed continuously regardless of whether lactose is present or not. Even in glucose-only medium (no lactose), the structural genes will be expressed constitutively because the negative regulatory mechanism (repressor binding to operator) is completely absent. This demonstrates that the default state of the lac operon without the repressor is 'on', and the repressor is needed to keep the operon switched off.
topic: Gene Regulation (Lac Operon) and Human Genome Project
subject: biology

options.text: The structural genes will not be transcribed because glucose represses the operon completely
The structural genes will be transcribed only if lactose is added to the medium
The structural genes will be transcribed constitutively even without lactose
The structural genes will be transcribed at a reduced rate compared to wild type in lactose medium

elimination_hints.hint: While glucose does influence the lac operon through catabolite repression (positive regulation via CAP-cAMP), the absence of the i gene means no repressor is made. Without the repressor, the operon cannot be switched off by the negative regulatory mechanism, so basal transcription occurs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the role of glucose repression and ignoring the primary effect of i gene deletion

elimination_hints.hint: Without the i gene, no repressor protein is produced. Since there is no repressor to block transcription, the structural genes do not need lactose (or allolactose) to be expressed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to understand that the inducer is only needed to inactivate the repressor, which is absent in this mutant

elimination_hints.hint: Without the repressor, the operon is constitutively on, not reduced. The expression would be continuous regardless of lactose presence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly predicting reduced expression when the negative regulator is absent
